title: ProtocolLab gRPC over HTTP/2 Contract Architecture
purpose: Describe a reproducible protobuf service and exact HTTP/2 binding while keeping implementations and languages outside the contract.
design_summary: The v2 deterministic EchoService contract defines all four RPC cardinalities plus bounded trailers-only, deadline, cancellation, gzip, and fixed-metadata methods. Scenario variants cover empty, 128-byte, 1 KiB, and 1 MiB payloads and separate reused-channel RPC measurement from new-channel establishment-plus-RPC measurement. Protobuf scenarios use application/grpc+proto as the canonical request and response representation while admitting the standards-equivalent application/grpc response emitted by conforming runtimes; the exact observed response media type remains evidence. HTTP/2 negotiation, message hashes, expected terminal status, and retry suppression are mandatory validity evidence.
key_components: gRPC smoke, comparison, diagnostic, and lifecycle suites
data_and_state_considerations: Request and response message sequences are deterministic. Identity coding pins payload, protobuf, and envelope hashes. Gzip validation compares the decompressed protobuf message because conforming gzip encoders need not emit byte-identical streams. Fixed metadata uses one ASCII and one decoded binary value. Expected deadline and cancellation outcomes are diagnostic successes, not ranking failures.
edge_cases_and_constraints: The accepted protobuf response media types are exactly application/grpc and application/grpc+proto; application/grpc+json and gRPC-Web media types are excluded.
Trailers-only INVALID_ARGUMENT, client deadline, and client cancellation are separate terminal semantics.
Channel creation time is not mixed into reused-channel RPC latency.
Gzip wire bytes are recorded but are not a comparison key.
alternatives_considered: A language-specific generated service was rejected.
One generic streaming scenario was rejected because server-streaming and bidirectional flow have different completion semantics.
risks: Different protobuf serializers can emit different bytes unless the serialized message contract is pinned.
HTTP/2 fallback can be hidden by client libraries unless negotiated protocol proof is retained.
Client libraries can synthesize deadline or cancellation status locally, so evidence must identify the initiating event and observed stream termination.
Compression ratios are not comparable unless encoder identity and settings are matched.
